nameMeanings.name: Skelton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Yorkshire): habitational name mainly from Skelton in Cumberland, or any of various places called Skelton in Yorkshire, North Yorkshire, and East Yorkshire. The placenames probably all derive from Old English scelf ‘rock, ledge, shelf’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’, with initial Sk-, instead of Sh-, due to Scandinavian influence. However, in some cases, the first element may be a lost river name, from Old Norse skjallr ‘resounding’; compare Skelding . This English surname has been established in Queen's County [Laois], Ireland, since the 16th century.

variants.name: Skelding
meanings: English: habitational name from Skelding (Yorkshire). The place takes its name from the river Skell, itself from Old Norse skjallr ‘resounding’, + Old English denu ‘valley’. In addition, the name may come from Skeldon (Ayrshire), although there is a lack of evidence for a historic presence in this part of Scotland.

variants.name: Skilton
meanings: English (Surrey): probably a variant of Skelton , though in some cases the name may be a variant of Shilton, a habitational name from Shilton (Warwickshire, Oxfordshire) or Earl Shilton (Leicestershire), from Old English scelf, scylf ‘rock, ledge, shelf’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’.

nameMeanings.name: Edward
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

en.lifeSummary: Edward Skelton was born in 1252, in York, Yorkshire, England as the son of Sir Richard Skelton. He had at least 1 son and 1 daughter with Mrs. Edward Skelton. He died after 1298, in Skelton, North Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom.

ko.lifeSummary: Edward Skelton(은)는 1252년 영국 요르크실르 요르크에서 Sir Richard Skelton의 아들로 태어났다. 그는 Mrs. Edward Skelton(와)과 적어도 한 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1298년 이후 영국 잉글랜드 노르스 요르크실르 스켈턴에서 사망했다.
